speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four, Lola Putt. Lola, why do you think you survived the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: If somebody got luck in life, and you... and God wants you to live, you can't go against that wish, can you?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is important to you today in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to be healthy. I want my children to be healthy and happy, and to see them grow. My grandchildren. My children. To see-- I'm enjoying my life now what never I enjoyed before. I'm getting older. I'm sure everybody else gets older. But I'm enjoying see them growing up. But behind my back, always I'm scared, Pauline. I have always this fear. That fear is still inside me. Like I said to you before, I'll repeat it again. I'm sorry I have to repeat it. I did not want to do this interview, because I was scared. And I was scared and painful, too, to tell you this story- this story. But I am very happy to take this out from my heart, this- this this pain that I have for so many years. To share it with you, and to share it with the world. To know that never happen again, and not to forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you like to give future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want the generation to think, and believe, what what your parents been through in life. Because here, it's a beautiful country, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I have everything. We are giving our children what we didn't have before. Maybe we spoiled them by giving them things. Because we know how much we've been through, what we didn't have, and we want our children to have everything. But I don't want them to take life for granted. I want- want them to remember. What I wish, Pauline-- I'm glad you asked me this question-- I wish that my children, and my grandchildren, and their grandchildren, time to time to look at this video, what we did today, for many years to come. And to remember what I've been through in my life, and not to be forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: How important is Judaism to you, Lola?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Jewish in heart. Very much, indeed. And I am what I am, what God made me. And I believe that the whole world is one. I don't think anybody any different than myself. God put us here for us to live together in peace. But you can see it's not that way. But we have to try. We have to learn to- to live together peacefully. And I wish the generation to understand it's not always milk and honey life. They have to remember, time to time, that our parents been through so much, and that, and that. And that we should behave different. We should treat people different. We should understand each other. And it will be a wonderful world to live in if they do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to add to your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: You ask me something, and I will answer you. For the moment being, what it was in my heart, I said. And I hope that, like I said before, I want my children and the whole world to see this this this video. That I am not just telling a story here. Do I do I look like I'm telling a story here? I'm saying what I've been through in my life. And I want [INAUDIBLE] people to believe me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, I want to thank you. It was a privilege. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for listen to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Lola, can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how old he was at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 45.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: When when the Germans sent us away in Auschwitz, he was about 45.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had it hanging on the wall, and my sister took it. And because she was hiding from the Germans, and she had it with her. And she make a copy and she gave me one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Lola, can you tell me about this photograph? Who are the people in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Maria-- or, Rachel, I mean. She got two names. Rachel and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one with the with the crown. I always want to look like the queen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is it that Rachel has two names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in the war, she changed just the name to call herself Maria, because she was very scared to say Rachel, because Rachel is a Jewish name. And Maria, she thought to say to herself that she is not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how is she known today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, she's still got the Rachel and Maria. Both- both- both names she kept it. Her husband is English, and she married him in- in Greece. When the war was finished, the English soldiers came to Greece. And my sister was very poor. She didn't have anything. So he gave her to eat and paid the bill for the for the room where she was staying. And so they fell in love and they married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you have this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had it, and she-- and I took it from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about this photo, Lola?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is-- the middle boy is my brother, Morris. The other two, they are friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is Morris in this photo?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come by this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: You can see this photograph is a little bit damaged, because it's a very old photograph. But my sister, because she's older than me, she always kept the photographs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what year this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. He had beautiful hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Lola, can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is there to say? I'm looking at now. [BACKROUND NOISE] It's wonderful to see that I still got this photograph. I was 14 in that picture with my sister. We were going for a walk where the where the sea is, you know? For walks, we used to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the one with the bow in my hair, and the other one is my sister, because she's older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you have this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe my sister gave it to me. Everything I can say, is because of my sister, I've got all those photographs. Because I didn't really have nothing on me or to go with me. She- she she likes always to keep photographs.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's lovely.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, when was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken after the war in Belgium, when I went to Belgium. And that was maybe about a year after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Lola, who are the people in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the people who took me in, when I told you before, that that man saw me in the street when I was crying, and he took me into his house. This is his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, to be honest. I don't remember. And this is his wife, and this is me when I was living with them. And then, when I met my husband, because this is the nephew from those two nice people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Lola, can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my husband and myself. We were not married then. After the war, we- we met at this place that I show you before, where I was staying with that couple that took me in. And this is the nephew. And we went shopping together. And on the streets somebody took us a picture. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same year, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moniac.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Lola, can you tell me about this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was my wedding day. And behind, that's my husband. And behind them is the nephew of the couple I told you about it that I was living with them. And the other man is Samuel from this camp. And I was very happy that day. I didn't marry in white, but never mind. And that was my happiest day of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: That that was all happening in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everything, what you saw here, Pauline, all of them was 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, when was this photo taken, and who are the people in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken 1956. That is my husband, Morry, and myself, and Greta, and Harry in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excellent. When was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was on my son's bar mitzvah in [INAUDIBLE]. My husband, Greta, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. When was this photo taken, Lola?

speaker: interviewee
text: Greta's wedding. And-

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are the people in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is her husband, myself, and my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ben.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wajsman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my husband in the middle. In the back is my son, myself, next to Harry. Next to my husband is Greta. Then is Diane, and Kiarra, their daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Lola, what was the occasion of this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my grandson's bar mitzvah, Jarrod, 1991. This is Harry, my son, his wife, Diane, Kiarra, and Tynan, and myself, next to Harry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's lovely. Thank you very much, Lola.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's finished?

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, would you like to introduce the members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. This is Greta, my oldest daughter. A wonderful daughter. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is Loni, my granddaughter. This is my grandson, Troy. And this is my partner, Garry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Greta, would you like to say a few words? Who would like to say a few words, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll speak to my nana. This is my Nana Putt. She's a wonderful nana. Her spirit enriches our lives. Her survival skills got her through the war, and we're proud of her for telling her story today. And I hope we enjoy many more years together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, Loni. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Troy, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad my nana is doing this video, so the history of her survival can be kept forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Greta, you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All I'd like to say is that I think you have probably got a more in-depth-- what is the name-- account of her life than we ever had. Because we've always had snippets, but never three hours of what you've got, of what she's went through in the war. She has been a wonderful mother. She's- she loves life. And I think she appreciates it more because of what she went through. And I wish her many, many years of good health, that we can enjoy good times together in the future.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lola, once again, on behalf of the Foundation, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for having us.
